Homework 7

Anthony Ferraro

Filtering the Diamonds Dataset

filtered_diamonds <- diamonds %>%
  filter(color %in% c("D", "G", "J")) %>%
  filter(clarity %in% c("I1", "VS1", "IF"))

knitr::kable(filtered_diamonds %>%
               count(color, clarity) %>%
               arrange(desc(n))
             )
color clarity n
G VS1 2148
D VS1 705
G IF 681
J VS1 542
G I1 150
D IF 73
J IF 51
J I1 50
D I1 42

1

Plotting the Relationship Between Price and Carat

ggplot(filtered_diamonds, aes(x = carat, y = price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

There seems to be a positive relationship between carat and price, which does make sense. Also, the relationship does seem to be linear. 2

Plotting the Relationship Between Price and Carat with Diamond Color

ggplot(filtered_diamonds, aes(x = carat, y = price, color = color)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

The relationship between carat and price is still positive and linear, however, it seems that some diamond colors have steeper price increases than others. 3

Plotting the Relationship Between Price and Carat with Diamond Color and Diamond Clarity

ggplot(filtered_diamonds, aes(x = carat, y = price, color = color, shape = clarity, 
                              linetype = clarity)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

I think that adding clarity in this way makes the graph harder to read as there are too many different shapes, colors, and line types on the graph. 4

Plotting the Relationship Between Price and Carat with Diamond Color and Clarity Faceted

ggplot(filtered_diamonds, aes(x = carat, y = price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") + 
  facet_grid(color ~ clarity)

Most of the carat vs. price relationships are still linear, although some look like they are almost exponential. The steepest relationship is between D color and IF clarity, which makes sense because they are the best for their respective categories. 5

Finding the Highest Price Per Carat Ratio

knitr::kable(filtered_diamonds %>%
  mutate(price_per_carat = (price / carat)) %>%
  group_by(color, clarity) %>%
  summarize(avg_price_pc = mean(price_per_carat)) %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_price_pc))
)
color clarity avg_price_pc
D IF 9937.419
G VS1 4420.881
D VS1 4204.891
G IF 4007.010
J VS1 3750.351
J IF 3220.227
D I1 3064.485
J I1 2710.080
G I1 2617.478

The table does seem to agree with my plots, with the D color and IF clarity combination being the highest price per carat, and the G color and I1 clarity combination being the lowest price per carat. 6
